title: My Year as an Au Pair
summary: A young British woman remembers her year working as an au pair for a German family in Munich — the language, the food, and the two small girls who taught her more than school ever did.
body: Before I started university, I spent a year as an au pair with a family in Munich. I was nineteen and very nervous on the first day. My German was almost zero. The family had two daughters, Lena and Emma, who were six and four years old. The parents both worked long hours, so I looked after the girls in the afternoons. I walked them home from school, helped with homework, and made small snacks. In the beginning, the girls spoke faster than I could understand. They laughed at my mistakes, but in a kind way. By the third month, I was already dreaming in German sometimes. The food was different from home, too. I had never eaten warm potato salad before, or so much bread. We went to the Christmas market together in December, and the parents bought me my first cup of hot wine. I was a little embarrassed because everyone seemed to know each other and I didn't. But Lena held my hand the whole time. When I left in August the next year, both girls cried at the airport. So did I. Years later, I still speak German with their accent. I have visited them twice, and they still call me their 'English sister.'

annotations.meaningVi: người trông trẻ ở cùng gia đình bản xứ (au pair)
annotations.exampleEn: She worked as an au pair in France for one year.
I was very nervous before the exam.
She looked after her younger brother every evening.

annotations.meaningVi: giúp làm bài tập về nhà
annotations.exampleEn: My father helped me with my homework.
She corrected my mistakes in a kind way.

annotations.meaningVi: mơ bằng tiếng Đức (dấu hiệu thông thạo)
annotations.exampleEn: After six months, I started dreaming in German.
She served warm potato salad with sausages.
The Christmas market opens in late November.
We drank hot wine to keep warm.
I felt a little embarrassed at the party.
He still speaks Italian with their accent.

grammarNotes.title: Past simple narrative vs present perfect closing
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Phần lớn câu chuyện dùng quá khứ đơn để kể lại các sự kiện đã kết thúc. Cuối bài chuyển sang hiện tại hoàn thành để nhấn mạnh trải nghiệm vẫn còn ý nghĩa đến nay: 'I have visited them twice'.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The story is told mostly in Past Simple (started, was, looked after, walked, laughed). The ending switches to Present Perfect to show experiences still relevant now: 'I have visited them twice.'

grammarNotes.title: Past perfect for 'before' events
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Dùng quá khứ hoàn thành (had + V3) để chỉ một việc đã xảy ra trước một thời điểm khác trong quá khứ. Trong bài: 'I had never eaten warm potato salad before' — trước thời điểm đó cô chưa từng ăn món này.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Use Past Perfect (had + past participle) for events earlier than another past moment. From the text: 'I had never eaten warm potato salad before.'

translationVi: Trước khi vào đại học, tôi đã sống một năm với vai trò au pair trong một gia đình ở Munich. Khi đó tôi mười chín tuổi và rất hồi hộp ngày đầu tiên. Tiếng Đức của tôi gần như bằng không. Gia đình có hai cô con gái, Lena và Emma, sáu và bốn tuổi. Bố mẹ đều làm việc nhiều giờ, nên tôi trông hai bé vào buổi chiều. Tôi đón các bé từ trường về, giúp làm bài tập, và làm vài món ăn vặt nhỏ. Lúc đầu, các bé nói nhanh hơn tôi có thể hiểu. Chúng cười khi tôi sai, nhưng theo cách tử tế. Đến tháng thứ ba, đôi khi tôi đã mơ bằng tiếng Đức. Đồ ăn cũng khác quê tôi. Tôi chưa bao giờ ăn salad khoai tây ấm, hay nhiều bánh mì đến vậy. Tháng Mười Hai cả nhà đi chợ Giáng sinh, và bố mẹ mua cho tôi cốc rượu nóng đầu tiên trong đời. Tôi hơi ngại vì mọi người dường như đều quen nhau còn tôi thì không. Nhưng Lena cứ nắm tay tôi suốt. Khi tôi rời đi vào tháng Tám năm sau, cả hai bé khóc ở sân bay. Tôi cũng khóc. Nhiều năm sau, tôi vẫn nói tiếng Đức với giọng của hai bé. Tôi đã thăm gia đình họ hai lần, và hai bé vẫn gọi tôi là 'chị gái người Anh'.

questions.prompt: How old was the narrator when she started?
questions.explanation: 'I was nineteen and very nervous on the first day.'
questions.hint: Listen for her age at the start.

questions.prompt: How much German did she know at the beginning?
questions.hint: Listen for her language level at the start.

questions.prompt: What are the daughters' names and ages?
questions.explanation: 'Lena and Emma, who were six and four years old.'
questions.hint: Listen carefully for the order of names and ages.

questions.prompt: When did the narrator look after the girls?
questions.explanation: 'I looked after the girls in the afternoons.'
questions.hint: Listen for the time of day she had care of them.

questions.prompt: How did the girls react to her language mistakes?
options: They laughed at her, but in a kind way
questions.explanation: 'They laughed at my mistakes, but in a kind way.'
questions.hint: Listen for two things: their action AND the manner.

questions.prompt: By when had she started dreaming in German?
options: By the end of the year
questions.explanation: 'By the third month, I was already dreaming in German sometimes.'

questions.prompt: Which food was new to her?
questions.explanation: 'I had never eaten warm potato salad before.'
questions.hint: Listen for an unusual side dish.

questions.prompt: Where did the family go in December?
questions.explanation: 'We went to the Christmas market together in December.'
questions.hint: Listen for a famous winter outing.

questions.prompt: Why did she feel a little embarrassed?
options: Everyone seemed to know each other and she didn't
questions.explanation: 'everyone seemed to know each other and I didn't.'
questions.hint: Listen for the reason behind her feeling.

questions.prompt: What happened at the airport when she left?
options: Both girls cried, and so did the narrator
questions.explanation: 'both girls cried at the airport. So did I.'

questions.prompt: What does the narrator say is still true years later?
options: She still speaks German with the girls' accent, and they call her their 'English sister'
questions.explanation: 'I still speak German with their accent… they still call me their English sister.'
questions.hint: Listen for the final two facts.

cloze.template: Before I started university, I spent a year as an au {{b1}} with a family in Munich. I was nineteen and very nervous on the first day. My German was almost {{b2}}. The family had two daughters, Lena and Emma, who were six and four years old. The parents both worked long hours, so I {{b3}} after the girls in the afternoons. I walked them home from school, helped with {{b4}}, and made small snacks. In the beginning, the girls spoke faster than I could understand. They {{b5}} at my mistakes, but in a kind way. By the third month, I was already {{b6}} in German sometimes. The food was different from home, too. I had never eaten warm {{b7}} salad before, or so much bread. We went to the Christmas {{b8}} together in December, and the parents bought me my first cup of hot wine. I was a little {{b9}} because everyone seemed to know each other and I didn't. But Lena held my hand the whole time. When I left in August the next year, both girls cried at the airport. So did I. Years later, I still speak German with their {{b10}}. I have visited them twice, and they still call me their 'English sister.'

blanks.explanation: 'pair' — completes 'au pair', a specific job title.
'dreaming' — a sign of becoming fluent in a language.
'potato' — warm potato salad, a German classic.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The narrator says the two little girls taught her more than school ever did. Do you think you learn a language better from textbooks, or from living with the people who speak it every day? What can children teach an adult learner that a teacher cannot?
